workflow.name: Scan business cards to Google Sheets and iPhone contacts with Telegram and easybits
workflow.description:
### What This Workflow Does

Snap a photo of a business card – or a whole stack laid out on a desk – and get back a Google Sheets row for every contact plus a `.vcf` file you can tap to add directly to your iPhone

Built for the moment you come back from a conference with 30 cards in your pocket. Lay them out, take one photo, and your contacts are in your phone before you finish your coffee.

---

### How it works

1. **Telegram bot** receives a photo of one or more business cards
2. **easybits Extractor** reads every card in the image and returns structured contact data (name, title, company, email, phone, website, address, notes)
3. **Deduplication check** compares each new contact against existing rows in the sheet – already-saved contacts are filtered out automatically
4. **Google Sheets** gets a new row for every new contact
5. **Individual vCard files** are sent back via Telegram, one per new contact – tap any of them to add directly to your phone

---

### What you need

- An n8n instance (Cloud or self-hosted)
- A Telegram bot (free, takes 2 minutes to create via @BotFather)
- A Google Sheet with these column headers: `timestamp` · `name` · `title` · `company` · `email` · `phone` · `website` · `address` · `notes`
- An easybits account at [easybits.tech](https://easybits.tech) – your Pipeline ID and API Key from the pipeline details page

---

### Setting up the easybits Extractor in n8n

The easybits Extractor is a **verified community node**, so it's available on n8n Cloud out of the box – just search for "easybits Extractor" in the node panel and start using it. No installation needed.

On a **self-hosted instance**, go to **Settings → Community Nodes → Install** and enter: `@easybits/n8n-nodes-extractor`

Once added to your canvas, you only need two values from your easybits pipeline details page:

- **Pipeline ID**
- **API Key**

Leave **Input Type** on `Binary Files` (the default) – it reads the photo binary directly from the Telegram node, no encoding step needed.

---

### Dedup logic

Contacts are matched by **email** when available, otherwise by **name + company**. If a contact already exists in the sheet, it won't be re-added or sent back as a vCard – only genuinely new contacts come through.

workflow.name: Business Card Scanner – Telegram to Sheets + vCard (powered by easybits)

additionalFields.caption:
=📇 {{ $json.name }}{{ $json.title ? ' — ' + $json.title : '' }}{{ $json.company ? ' @ ' + $json.company : '' }}

Tap to add to your contacts.

parameters.content:
## 📱 New Photo from Telegram

Fires when someone sends a photo to your bot – whether it's a single card or a desk shot of 30 cards.

**Setup:** Connect your Telegram credential and set Updates to `message`. The `download: true` flag tells n8n to pull the photo binary automatically.

## 🔍 Extract Contacts (easybits)

The extractor reads the photo and returns an **array of contacts** – one object per card visible in the image. The prompt asks for `name`, `title`, `company`, `email`, `phone`, `website`, `address`, and `notes`, with explicit null handling for missing fields.

**Why an array?** The same workflow handles 1 card or 30 cards in a single photo – easybits detects each card as a distinct rectangular object and returns one entry per card.

## 📋 Split Into Individual Contacts

The extractor output looks like `{ contacts: [ {...}, {...}, {...} ] }`. This node splits the array so each contact becomes its own n8n item — needed for per-row sheet writes and per-contact vCards downstream.

**Field to split:** `data.contacts`

## 🔑 Add Match Key (New) & Add Match Key (Existing)

Creates a `match_key` field on each contact for deduplication.

**Logic:** uses `email` if present, otherwise falls back to `name|company`. Lowercased and trimmed for consistent matching across cases like `Sarah@Acme.com` vs `sarah@acme.com`.

**Both sides** (incoming contacts + existing rows) need the same key built the exact same way – that's why this node exists twice.

## 📊 Read Existing Contacts

Pulls every row from the Sheet so we can compare what's new vs. what's already saved. Runs in parallel with the extraction branch – both start from the Telegram Trigger and meet at the Merge node.

**Note:** This reads the full sheet on every run. Fine up to a few thousand rows; consider a database for larger contact stores.

## 🚫 Filter Out Duplicates

Compares incoming contacts against existing rows by `match_key` and outputs **only the new ones**.

**Mode:** Combine by Matching Fields  
**Output:** Keep Non-Matches from Input 1

If a contact's match_key already exists in the sheet, it gets dropped here – no duplicate row, no duplicate vCard.

## 📝 Save to Google Sheets

One row per new contact. Phone arrays get joined into a comma-separated string. Cell format is set to `RAW` so phone numbers starting with `+` aren't misinterpreted as formulas.

**Sheet headers required:**  
`timestamp` · `name` · `title` · `company` · `email` · `phone` · `website` · `address` · `notes`

## 📇 Build vCard File

Builds a vCard 3.0 `.vcf` file for each contact – runs once per item.

vCard 3.0 is broadly compatible with iOS Contacts, Android, macOS Contacts, and Outlook. Special characters in addresses (commas, semicolons) are escaped properly, and missing fields are skipped cleanly rather than producing empty lines.

**Mode:** Run Once for Each Item

## 📤 Send vCard to Telegram

Sends each `.vcf` file as a Telegram document with a labeled caption (`📇 Sarah Jones – Head of Sales @ Acme Corp`).

**On iPhone:** tap the file → "Add Contact" → done.  
**On Android:** opens directly in Contacts app.

The chat ID is passed through from the Code node (`$json.chat_id`) rather than referenced upstream – this avoids item-linking issues that can break when binary data is involved.

## 📇 Business Card Scanner
(powered by easybits)

**Snap a photo of a business card – or a whole stack laid out on a desk – and get back a Google Sheets row for every contact plus a `.vcf` file you can tap to add directly to your iPhone.**

Built for the moment you come back from a conference with 30 cards in your pocket. Lay them out, take one photo, and your contacts are in your phone before you finish your coffee.

### How it works
1. **Telegram bot** receives a photo of one or more business cards
2. **easybits Extractor** reads every card in the image and returns structured contact data (name, title, company, email, phone, website, address, notes)
3. **Deduplication check** compares each new contact against existing rows in the sheet – already-saved contacts are filtered out automatically
4. **Google Sheets** gets a new row for every new contact
5. **Individual vCard files** are sent back via Telegram, one per new contact – tap any of them to add directly to your phone

### What you need
- An n8n instance (Cloud or self-hosted)
- A Telegram bot (free, takes 2 minutes to create via @BotFather)
- A Google Sheet with these column headers: `timestamp` · `name` · `title` · `company` · `email` · `phone` · `website` · `address` · `notes`
- An easybits account at [easybits.tech](https://easybits.tech) – your Pipeline ID and API Key from the pipeline details page

### Setting up the easybits Extractor in n8n
The easybits Extractor is a **verified community node**, so it's available on n8n Cloud out of the box – just search for "easybits Extractor" in the node panel and start using it. No installation needed.

On a **self-hosted instance**, go to **Settings → Community Nodes → Install** and enter: `@easybits/n8n-nodes-extractor`

Once added to your canvas, you only need two values from your easybits pipeline details page:

- **Pipeline ID**
- **API Key**

Leave **Input Type** on `Binary Files` (the default) – it reads the photo binary directly from the Telegram node, no encoding step needed.

### Dedup logic
Contacts are matched by **email** when available, otherwise by **name + company**. If a contact already exists in the sheet, it won't be re-added or sent back as a vCard – only genuinely new contacts come through.
